title: The gender wage ratio in different income percentiles
subtitle: The gender ratio refers to the raw female-male wage ratio. "Ninetieth percentile" indicates an income higher than that of 90% of the population - thus, for example, in 2010 a 74% ratio in the ninetieth percentile indicates that the highest earning women made 74% of the hourly wage of the highest earning men.
caption:
The gender wage ratio in different income percentiles
The gender ratio refers to the raw female-male wage ratio. "Ninetieth percentile" indicates an income higher than that of 90% of the population - thus, for example, in 2010 a 74% ratio in the ninetieth percentile indicates that the highest earning women made 74% of the hourly wage of the highest earning men.

The chart tracks the gender wage ratio across three income percentiles in the United States from 1980 to 2010. All three lines show an upward trend over the period, indicating that the ratio of female to male wages increased at each level. The tenth percentile line, shown in blue, begins near 65% in 1980 and rises steadily to just above 80% by 2010. The fiftieth percentile line, in red, starts at approximately 60% in 1980 and climbs to roughly 82% by the end of the period, crossing above the tenth percentile line around 1998. The ninetieth percentile line, in brown, begins near 62% in 1980, rises to about 75% by 1990, then flattens out and remains relatively stable between 74% and 75% through 2010. By 2010, the fiftieth and tenth percentiles converge near the 80% mark, while the ninetieth percentile remains lower at approximately 74%. The gap between the highest earners (ninetieth percentile) and the middle earners (fiftieth percentile) narrows considerably from 1980 to 1990, after which the ninetieth percentile line plateaus while the other two continue their ascent.
